teaching_arc.entry_point: Begin by connecting directly to Session 3: students already know how to inspect nodes, topics, parameters, and the ROS graph; today those tools make a real Panda manipulator model visible.
teaching_arc.progression: Start with why Panda matters, establish the URDF/Xacro to robot_description to joint states to TF to RViz pipeline, inspect installed Panda resources, build a small launch package, configure and save RViz, then debug the running graph and TF tree.
teaching_arc.destination: Students should finish able to launch Panda in RViz2, explain why it appears, move joints with sliders, inspect joint states and TF, and diagnose a blank RViz scene in a disciplined order.

audience_level: undergraduate robotics lab students who know basic ROS 2 graph inspection and now need to connect robot description, joint states, TF, and RViz2 using the Panda manipulator.
topic_goal: Make the Panda robot model real in ROS 2: students should understand the model-to-visualization pipeline well enough to launch it, inspect it, and debug it before using MoveIt.
topic_takeaways: Students should know that URDF/Xacro describes robot structure: links, joints, geometry, and frames.
Students should understand that robot_description is the model text consumed by robot_state_publisher and visualization tools.
Students should know that joint states plus the robot model produce TF, and TF is what lets RViz place every Panda link correctly.
Students should be able to build a small launch package that starts robot_state_publisher, Joint State Publisher GUI, and RViz2.
Students should debug Panda visualization in order: environment, package visibility, launch file, nodes, robot_description, joint states, TF, then RViz Fixed Frame and displays.
Students should see this session as the foundation for MoveIt: planning comes after the robot model and frame system are trustworthy.
style_notes: Speak like a live lab instructor guiding students through a real setup, not like a manual reading commands aloud.
For command-heavy slides, explain the purpose, the one or two command names students should notice, and the expected verification signal; do not recite every character.
When a path or command appears, say it naturally for TTS: for example, opt Ross humble setup dot bash, robot description, joint states, T F, T F static, and Panda link zero.
Keep returning to the central pipeline: Panda model, robot_description, joint states, robot_state_publisher, TF, and RViz.
Make the live-lab pauses explicit: tell students when to check their folder, save the file, build, source, launch, or inspect a result before moving on.
Do not teach MoveIt deeply in this session. Use MoveIt only as the reason this model and frame foundation matters.
transition_style: Use each slide as one step in a lab story: first make the purpose clear, then show the exact action or concept, then say what should be true before the next slide.

slide_ref.source_title: Session 4 - Panda Robot Description, TF, and RViz2

slides.narration_seed: Welcome students into Session 4 by saying this is where Panda first becomes visible. Remind them that Session 3 taught graph inspection, and today those tools explain a robot model instead of a toy example.
must_say: Today is model and frames, not motion planning yet.
slides.transition_from_previous: Welcome students back and connect directly to Session 3: the same graph inspection habits now apply to a real robot model.
slides.transition_to_next: Move from the welcome into why Panda is the right robot to standardize on before MoveIt.
slides.explanation_style: Frame the whole session as building the model and frame foundation, not as motion planning yet.
slides.story_hint: Open like the beginning of a real lab: Session 3 gave the graph tools, and today the Panda robot becomes visible for the first time.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should know that today is about making Panda visible and explainable before any planning happens.

slides.narration_seed: Explain why Panda is the course robot for this stage: it is the MoveIt tutorial robot, it has seven degrees of freedom, and the same names will return later.
must_cover: Panda is the official MoveIt Humble tutorial robot.
It is a 7-DOF manipulator, so it prepares students for redundancy and null-space ideas later.
The same names will return: panda_link0, panda_arm, robot_description, /joint_states, and /tf.
must_say: Panda is not a throwaway demo robot; it is the robot we reuse for MoveIt.
slides.transition_from_previous: Connect the previous slide to the central reason this lab matters for the rest of the course.
slides.transition_to_next: Use this slide to prepare the next step in the pipeline rather than treating the bullets as isolated facts.
likely_student_confusion: Students may think the goal is simply to see a robot picture, when the real goal is to understand the data path that made the picture appear.
slides.explanation_style: Use the visual or checklist as a roadmap, then return attention to the model-to-RViz pipeline.
slides.story_hint: Keep the professor focused on the lab destination: Panda in RViz, with students able to explain every data step that made it appear.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand the route through the lab and why Panda, robot_description, joint states, TF, and RViz belong together.

slide_ref.source_title: What Must Work By The End

slides.narration_seed: Treat this as the lab contract. Students should listen for the exact evidence that proves Panda is installed, launched, visible, movable, and inspectable.
must_cover: Install/check Panda description resources and visualization tools.
Find and inspect Panda URDF/Xacro, meshes, and MoveIt config folders.
Launch robot_state_publisher, Joint State Publisher GUI, and rviz2.
must_say: By the end, Panda should appear in RViz and the data path that made it appear should make sense.

slides.narration_seed: Make this the anchor diagram for the whole session. Say that nearly every failure today is one broken link in this model-to-RViz pipeline.
must_cover: URDF/Xacro describes the Panda links, joints, and geometry.
robot_description carries the processed robot model.
Joint State Publisher GUI publishes slider-controlled joint values as /joint_state_publisher.
must_say: Return to this pipeline whenever Panda is blank, frozen, or missing frames.

slides.narration_seed: Run this as the warm-up check. Say the goal is not speed; the goal is verifying the terminal is in a clean ROS Humble state before installing or launching anything.
must_cover: Expected distro: humble.
Expected workspace: ~/ros2_ws.
If package commands fail, source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash and your workspace overlay again.

must_cover: panda_description contains urdf/ and meshes/.
panda.urdf is the installed model we will launch today.

slides.narration_seed: Keep URDF conceptual: it is the robot structure model, not a planning algorithm and not merely XML syntax.
must_cover: URDF is the robot structure file.
A robot model is a tree of links connected by joints.
Each joint gives a transform from parent link to child link.
must_say: URDF describes robot structure; it is not motion planning.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use the previous setup or file-inspection step to motivate the concept rather than introducing it as abstract vocabulary.
slides.transition_to_next: Close the concept by naming how it will appear in the launch pipeline or RViz.
likely_student_confusion: Students may treat URDF, Xacro, links, joints, and TF as file syntax instead of a robot-structure and frame model.
slides.explanation_style: Explain the concept visually first using Panda, then give the ROS term students must remember.
slides.story_hint: Build the conceptual bridge from files to robot meaning: links and joints define structure, joint values define pose, and TF makes the pose visible.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to say what this concept does in the Panda visualization pipeline.
slides.scene_hint: Use the Panda diagram or frame tree to connect the term to the actual manipulator model, not to an unrelated robot example.

slides.narration_seed: Use the parent-link, joint, child-link diagram to make transforms concrete before TF is introduced formally.
must_cover: Link = rigid body such as a base, arm segment, wrist, hand, or finger.
Joint = relationship between a parent link and child link.
Origin = where the joint frame is placed relative to the parent.
must_say: A joint defines the transform relationship between a parent link and a child link.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use the previous setup or file-inspection step to motivate the concept rather than introducing it as abstract vocabulary.

slides.narration_seed: Students do not need to memorize every Panda frame, but panda_link0, panda_hand, and panda_arm should become familiar names.
must_cover: Base frame: panda_link0.
Arm links: panda_link1 through panda_link8.
Hand frame: panda_hand.

slides.narration_seed: Use Panda arm joints and gripper-style sliding as the intuitive examples for joint types.
must_cover: Fixed joints attach things that do not move.
Revolute joints rotate within limits; Panda arm joints use this idea.
Continuous joints rotate without limits; common in wheels.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use the previous setup or file-inspection step to motivate the concept rather than introducing it as abstract vocabulary.

slide_ref.source_title: Joint Values Make The Model Move

slides.narration_seed: Make the key connection before the GUI: joint sliders publish values, robot_state_publisher uses those values and the model, and RViz updates because TF updates.
must_cover: URDF gives structure, but not the current pose by itself.
/joint_states gives current joint values.
robot_state_publisher combines both to compute link transforms.
must_say: URDF gives the structure, joint states give the pose, and robot_state_publisher produces TF.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use the previous setup or file-inspection step to motivate the concept rather than introducing it as abstract vocabulary.
slides.transition_to_next: Close the concept by naming how it will appear in the launch pipeline or RViz.

slides.scene_hint: Use the Panda diagram or frame tree to connect the term to the actual manipulator model, not to an unrelated robot example.

slides.narration_seed: Prepare students for MoveIt by explaining why visual geometry and collision geometry have different jobs.
must_cover: Visual is what RViz draws.
Collision is what collision checkers and planners use.
Inertial is mass and inertia for physics simulation.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use the previous setup or file-inspection step to motivate the concept rather than introducing it as abstract vocabulary.

slide_ref.source_title: Visual Geometry Is Not Always Collision Geometry

slides.narration_seed: Use this example to warn that a robot can look correct while its collision model may still matter later for planning.
must_cover: Visual geometry can be detailed and pretty.
Collision geometry is often simpler for faster checking.
Do not debug planning from visuals alone.

slides.narration_seed: Explain Xacro as a way to keep long robot descriptions reusable. Do not turn this into advanced Xacro programming.
must_cover: Xacro is URDF with variables and macros.
It reduces copy-paste in long robot descriptions.
It helps manage meshes and package paths.

slides.narration_seed: Preview the idea that the launch file will pass model text into robot_description. The exact file path matters less than the parameter role.
must_cover: URDF input: XML robot model with links, joints, visuals, collisions, and inertials.
Launch file use: store that output in the robot_description parameter.
Later MoveIt use: Xacro files can generate the same kind of URDF string dynamically.
must_say: robot_description is the robot model text stored as a ROS parameter.

slides.narration_seed: Define TF as the live frame relationship system. Make /tf and /tf_static sound like data streams RViz uses, not abstract theory.
must_cover: TF answers pose questions: where is one frame relative to another?
/tf carries changing transforms.
/tf_static carries fixed transforms.
must_say: TF answers where one frame is relative to another.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use the previous setup or file-inspection step to motivate the concept rather than introducing it as abstract vocabulary.

slides.narration_seed: Turn the pipeline into the actual three tools the launch file starts today. Repeat that this is not MoveIt yet.
must_cover: robot_state_publisher reads robot_description and /joint_states, then publishes TF.
joint_state_publisher_gui executable opens sliders; the node appears as /joint_state_publisher.
rviz2 visualizes RobotModel and TF.
must_say: These nodes make model visualization work; they are not MoveIt yet.

slide_ref.source_title: Step 1: Create A Class Launch Package

slides.narration_seed: Start the hands-on package creation. Emphasize that our package wraps installed Panda resources instead of modifying them.
must_cover: Do not add runtime tools as CMake dependencies here.
This package only installs a launch file, so the CMake side should stay simple.

slides.transition_to_next: Before the next slide, remind students what file or folder should now exist.

slides.narration_seed: Pause to create the launch and rviz folders. This is a small setup step, but it keeps the package organized for reusable launch and config files.
must_cover: This package stores our launch file.
The rviz/ folder stores saved RViz layouts.
The Panda model still comes from the MoveIt resources package.
Expected files/folders: package.xml, CMakeLists.txt, launch/, and rviz/.

Runtime dependency means needed when launching, not needed for CMake to compile.

slides.narration_seed: Explain why this CMakeLists file stays minimal: it installs launch and RViz resources, while runtime dependencies remain in package.xml.

If the launch file is missing, you probably saved it in the wrong folder.

slides.narration_seed: Teach RViz setup by purpose: Fixed Frame establishes the reference, RobotModel draws Panda, and TF shows frame relationships.
must_cover: Set Fixed Frame to panda_link0.
Add RobotModel display to draw Panda.
Add TF display to see frames.
must_say: For this Panda demo, the Fixed Frame should be panda_link0.
I will switch to RViz for a moment so you can see exactly where this setting appears.
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from the launch result into the manual RViz setup needed to see Panda correctly.
slides.transition_to_next: After the RViz action, use the recorded desktop demo to show the fixed frame and RobotModel display before explaining why saving the config matters.
likely_student_confusion: Students may think a blank RViz window means the robot failed, when often the Fixed Frame or displays are simply not configured yet.
slides.explanation_style: Explain each RViz setting by what problem it solves, not as a menu tour.
slides.story_hint: Make RViz feel like an inspection instrument: Fixed Frame, RobotModel, TF display, then save the configuration so the setup is repeatable.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should configure RViz to show Panda and save a reusable RViz layout inside their package.
slides.scene_hint: Point to the RViz control or display that changes the visible Panda result without covering the command text.

slides.narration_seed: Make saving RViz config feel important: manual setup becomes reusable only after it is saved into the class package.
must_cover: In RViz2:
File -> Save Config As
Save to this exact path:
This saves the Fixed Frame, RobotModel display, TF display, and layout.

slides.narration_seed: Use the sliders as the live proof of the pipeline: move one joint, joint_states changes, TF changes, and Panda moves in RViz.
must_cover: Move one joint at a time so you can connect slider, joint, link, and frame.
Watch /joint_states change when a slider moves.
Watch TF update as robot_state_publisher recomputes link transforms.
Remember: the GUI is not a controller; it is a visualization/debugging tool.
must_say: The Joint State Publisher GUI is a visualization and debugging tool, not a real controller.
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from seeing the robot in RViz into inspecting the ROS data that makes it move.
slides.transition_to_next: After each inspection command, connect the observed topic, node, or frame to the central pipeline.
likely_student_confusion: Students may look at RViz only and forget to inspect the nodes, topics, and TF data underneath.
slides.explanation_style: Emphasize what question the command answers and what output confirms the pipeline is alive.
slides.story_hint: Return to Session 3 habits: now students inspect the live Panda pipeline instead of guessing why the model moves or fails.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should use node, topic, TF, rqt_graph, and rosbag tools to observe Panda model data deliberately.
slides.scene_hint: Connect the command output to the live Panda data path so students see why the inspection matters.

slide_ref.source_title: How To Read A TF Tree

slides.narration_seed: Use the TF tree visually. For Panda, students should expect a chain from base to wrist, hand, and fingers.
must_cover: Tree direction matters: parent frame -> child frame.
For Panda, you should see a chain from the base toward wrist/hand/fingers.
Missing branch usually means missing joint state or missing model data.

RViz subscribes to model/TF-related data to visualize the robot.
If the graph is confusing, hide debug nodes or refresh the graph.

slides.narration_seed: Keep rosbag optional. Present it as a way to capture hard-to-reproduce joint and TF behavior.
must_cover: Record while moving sliders.
Use bags when a frame or joint problem is hard to reproduce.
Do not record everything; start with the topics you need.

slides.narration_seed: This is the debugging ladder. Walk through it top to bottom and say explicitly that random guessing wastes time.
must_cover: Environment: ROS sourced, package visible.
Launch file: installed and found by ros2 launch.
Nodes: all three nodes are alive.
must_say: Debug in order: environment, package, launch, nodes, model, data, TF, then RViz.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use the inspection tools to build a disciplined debugging ladder instead of random fixes.
slides.transition_to_next: Close the debugging section by connecting the same foundation to MoveIt.
likely_student_confusion: Students may jump randomly between fixes instead of following the pipeline from source environment to RViz display.
slides.explanation_style: Sound firm and practical: start at environment and package visibility, then move downstream through launch, nodes, model, data, TF, and RViz.
slides.story_hint: Turn the whole session into a debugging order students can follow when RViz is blank or the sliders seem disconnected.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should leave with a reusable order for diagnosing Panda visualization failures.
slides.scene_hint: Use the debugging checklist as the main visual anchor and move through it top to bottom.

slides.narration_seed: Map each common error to the part of the pipeline it belongs to: package, launch install, CMake, model path, RViz display, joint states, or TF.
must_cover: package not found -> package not installed, not built, or workspace not sourced.
launch file not found -> missing CMake install rule or overlay not sourced.
CMake find_package error -> remove runtime GUI/tool packages from CMakeLists.txt.

slides.story_hint: Turn the whole session into a debugging order students can follow when RViz is blank or the sliders seem disconnected.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should leave with a reusable order for diagnosing Panda visualization failures.
slides.scene_hint: Use the debugging checklist as the main visual anchor and move through it top to bottom.

slides.narration_seed: Bridge to MoveIt without teaching it yet. The same Panda model, panda_link0, robot_description, and panda_arm return next time.
must_cover: MoveIt starts from the same robot model.
The MoveIt RViz plugin uses robot_description and Panda planning configuration.
The official quickstart sets Fixed Frame to panda_link0.
must_say: MoveIt starts from the same robot model and frame foundation.
slides.transition_from_previous: After debugging, show why this careful model work matters for the next planning session.
slides.transition_to_next: Move into the final checklist so students know what must be working before leaving.
likely_student_confusion: Students may expect MoveIt planning to solve model or TF problems that should be fixed before planning begins.
slides.explanation_style: Use the MoveIt image as a preview and repeatedly say that planning comes after a trustworthy model and TF tree.
slides.story_hint: Bridge forward to Session 5 without teaching planning yet: MoveIt uses the same Panda model and frame foundation.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand why Panda visualization is the prerequisite for MoveIt planning work.
slides.scene_hint: Use the MoveIt preview only as a forward bridge; do not start teaching planning details yet.

slides.narration_seed: Use this as the exit checklist. Students should know whether their own machine is ready before leaving.
must_cover: Panda resources visible: ros2 pkg prefix --share moveit_resources_panda_description works.
Class package builds: cb --packages-select mcg5353_panda_viz succeeds.
Launch works: ros2 launch mcg5353_panda_viz display_panda.launch.py starts RViz and GUI.
Students should verify RViz shows Panda, slider motion updates the model, and /joint_states, /tf, and /tf_static are inspectable.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use the final slides to turn the lab into memory and a readiness checklist.
slides.transition_to_next: Close the session by saying that next time the same Panda model becomes the basis for planning.
likely_student_confusion: Students may leave with the commands but not the pipeline memory needed for the next lab.
slides.explanation_style: Recap the pipeline in plain speech and end with a clear handoff to the next session.
slides.story_hint: Close like a real lab: ask students to verify their setup, name the five pieces of the pipeline, and prepare for MoveIt next time.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should leave knowing what should work on their machine and why those pieces matter for MoveIt.

slides.narration_seed: Close warmly and professionally. Summarize the five pieces: URDF/Xacro, robot_description, joint_states, TF, and RViz, then hand off to MoveIt next session.
must_cover: URDF/Xacro describes structure: links, joints, geometry, and frames.
robot_description is the model parameter that other tools consume.
/joint_states gives the current joint values.
TF gives frame relationships, and RViz uses those relationships to place every visible Panda link.
must_say: Today made the Panda model real in ROS; next we plan with it.
slides.transition_from_previous: Use the final slides to turn the lab into memory and a readiness checklist.
slides.transition_to_next: Close the session by saying that next time the same Panda model becomes the basis for planning.
likely_student_confusion: Students may leave with the commands but not the pipeline memory needed for the next lab.
slides.explanation_style: Recap the pipeline in plain speech and end with a clear handoff to the next session.
slides.story_hint: Close like a real lab: ask students to verify their setup, name the five pieces of the pipeline, and prepare for MoveIt next time.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should leave knowing what should work on their machine and why those pieces matter for MoveIt.
